title: Chocolate éclairs
subtitle: Choux pastry filled with vanilla pastry cream and glazed with chocolate
excerpt: Lengths of choux pastry baked until hollow, filled with cold vanilla crème pâtissière, and topped with a chocolate ganache glaze. The choux must be fully dried in the oven or the shells collapse on cooling. The pastry cream must be chilled thoroughly before piping or it softens the pastry from inside. Both can be prepared a day ahead.
sourceNotes: The éclair is a classic of French pâtisserie associated with the 19th-century Parisian pastry kitchen. The name (meaning lightning) appears in French cookbooks from the mid-1800s. The combination of choux pastry, pastry cream, and chocolate glaze was established in Parisian pastry shops before spreading to Britain through high-end confectioners in the late 19th century, becoming a popular home-baking project through the mid-20th century. The recipe covers both the choux pastry and the crème pâtissière (pastry cream) from scratch.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unfilled choux shells for up to 1 month. Re-crisp in a 200°C oven for 5 minutes from frozen before filling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry cream can be made a day ahead and refrigerated. The choux shells keep in an airtight container for 24 hours. Fill and glaze up to 3 hours before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 180°C for the choux. After 20 minutes at full temperature, reduce to 180°C conventional (160°C fan) for a further 10 to 15 minutes to dry the shells through. Do not open the oven in the first 20 minutes or the shells will collapse.

recipeTools.notes: two large baking trays, lined with baking paper
fitted with a 1.5 cm plain round nozzle for the choux and a smaller nozzle for filling

glossaryTerms.definition: A cooked paste of butter, water, flour, and eggs that puffs dramatically in the oven through steam pressure. The flour is added to boiling butter-water and beaten vigorously (the panade), then eggs are incorporated one at a time until the paste falls in a thick ribbon from a spoon. Too little egg and the choux will not puff; too much and it spreads flat. The shells must be fully dried in the oven after the initial puff — a small hole poked in the base of each lets steam escape and prevents collapse on cooling.

content.text: Éclairs require two separate preparations made independently: the

and the pastry cream. Both must be fully cool before assembly. The most common failure with choux is under-drying: the shells look done on the outside but are still steamy inside and collapse as they cool. When in doubt, leave them five minutes longer and poke a small hole in the base of each to release steam.

For the pastry cream, heat the milk in a saucepan until steaming. Whisk the egg yolks, sugar, flour, and cornflour together in a bowl. Pour the hot milk over the yolk mixture, whisking constantly. Return to the pan and cook over medium heat, stirring, until very thick and the mixture pulls from the sides. Stir in the vanilla and butter. Press clingfilm directly to the surface and refrigerate until fully cold.

Heat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan) and line two baking trays with baking paper. Bring the water and butter to a rolling boil in a saucepan. Add the sifted flour and salt all at once and beat vigorously with a wooden spoon until the paste pulls away from the sides. Cook for 1 minute, then remove from the heat and cool for 5 minutes. Beat in the eggs a little at a time until the paste is smooth and glossy and falls in a thick ribbon from a spoon. Transfer to a piping bag fitted with a 1.5 cm plain nozzle and pipe 12 cm lengths onto the trays. Bake for 20 minutes without opening the oven, then reduce to 180°C and bake for 10 to 15 minutes more until deep golden. Pierce the base of each éclair with a skewer and return to the oven for 5 minutes to dry. Cool completely on a rack.

For the glaze, pour the hot cream over the chopped chocolate and leave for 2 minutes. Stir from the centre until smooth and glossy. Transfer the cold pastry cream to a piping bag fitted with a small nozzle. Push the nozzle through the base of each éclair and pipe in the cream until the shell feels heavy. Dip the top of each éclair in the chocolate glaze and leave on the rack for 10 minutes to set before serving.
